did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning model, specifically EfficientNetB2, to perform automated skin cancer detection (classification) on dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2019, PH2, and a custom Asian dataset (SGH). The study evaluated the model's performance (AUC) across different racial groups to assess bias in AI-based dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training of an EfficientNetB2 deep learning model on the ISIC 2019 dataset and its evaluation on the PH2 and a custom SGH dataset to assess performance across racial groups. It reports specific quantitative results (AUC scores) and discusses methodology, limitations, and clinical relevance, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Exploring the Effect of Race in Automated Skin Cancer Detection
journal: 2025 47th Annual International Conference of the IEEE Engineering in Medicine and Biology Society (EMBC)
brief_summary: This study investigated the effect of race on automated skin cancer detection by evaluating an EfficientNetB2 deep learning model trained on predominantly Caucasian data against Asian skin. The model was trained on the ISIC 2019 dataset and evaluated using cross-validation on ISIC 2019, the PH2 dataset, and a custom dataset from Singapore General Hospital (SGH) consisting primarily of Asian patients. Performance was measured using the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUC) curve. The model achieved high AUC scores of 0.90 on ISIC 2019 and 0.91 on PH2, but performance dropped to 0.81 when tested on the Asian SGH dataset. The results indicate that AI models trained on Caucasian populations do not perform equally well on Asian skin of Fitzpatrick types II-III. The authors conclude that existing AI methods may not be optimized for Asian skin, highlighting the need for specific research and data representation for skin of color.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in ISIC 2019, PH2, and SGH datasets. Classified as skin cancer in the binary detection task.
Benign lesions included in ISIC 2019, PH2, and SGH datasets. Classified as normal/non-cancer in the binary detection task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer (presence vs. absence)
all_task_types.details: The model predicts the presence or absence of skin cancer (binary classification) using an EfficientNetB2 deep learning model. The output is a probability thresholded to determine the class. The task involves distinguishing between skin cancer classes (Melanoma, BCC, SCC, AK) and non-cancerous classes (Melanocytic nevus, Benign keratosis, etc.) aggregated into a binary 'skin cancer' vs 'normal/non-cancer' label.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (HAM10000/BCN20000 subset)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images contributed by institutions from Spain, Australia, Austria, USA, Turkey, New Zealand, Sweden, and Argentina. Inferred to be predominantly Caucasian. Used as the primary training dataset and for internal cross-validation.
classes_used: Benign keratosis (solar lentigo / seborrheic keratosis / lichen planus-like keratosis)

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNetB2 model from scratch.
use.validation: Used 20% of training data (every 5th sample) for validation during cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for internal evaluation via stratified five-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image database collected at the Dermatology Service of Hospital Pedro Hispano in Matosinhos, Portugal. Inferred to be predominantly Caucasian based on geography. Used for external validation on Caucasian skin.

use.testing: Used to test the model trained on ISIC 2019 to evaluate performance on a different Caucasian dataset.

datasets_used.name: SGH Dataset (Singapore General Hospital)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of dermoscopic images collected at Singapore General Hospital. Consists primarily of Asian patients (92 Chinese, 8 other ethnicities). Used to evaluate model performance on Asian skin.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning the model in a five-fold cross-validation setup (though results showed no improvement).
use.validation: Used for validation during fine-tuning (EarlyStopping callback).
use.testing: Used to test the model trained on ISIC 2019 to evaluate performance on Asian skin.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate model performance on the ISIC 2019 dataset and to split the SGH dataset for fine-tuning and testing.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure and compare the classification performance of the EfficientNetB2 model across different datasets (ISIC 2019, PH2, SGH) and racial groups.
To visualize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity for each fold of the cross-validation and to compute AUC values.
To summarize the overall performance across the five folds of cross-validation for each dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper states a hypothesis that AI models trained on Caucasian populations do not perform equally well on Asian skin, and reports that the results (AUC drop from 0.90/0.91 to 0.81) supported this hypothesis. However, the paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, p-values, significance levels) to validate this difference.

ISIC 2019 (Cross-Validation).metric: AUC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic)

EfficientNetB2.fold_details: All folds except fold one yielded an AUC of more than 0.9. Best fold AUC was 0.92 (fold 4).

ISIC 2019 (Cross-Validation).notes: The model learned the characteristics of the ISIC 2019 dataset well. Stratified five-fold cross-validation was used. Validation data consisted of 20% of the training data (every 5th sample).

PH2 Dataset.notes: The model was trained on ISIC 2019 and tested on PH2. Results were similar to ISIC 2019 cross-validation, indicating the model performed well on Caucasian skin.

SGH Dataset (Asian).notes: Performance dropped significantly compared to Caucasian datasets. Fine-tuning did not improve results, possibly due to small sample size (153 images, only 6 positive samples). The best AUC for the non-fine-tuned model was 0.84 (fold 4), which was still worse than the corresponding fold on ISIC 2019 (0.92).

architecture_used.pretraining: Trained from scratch on ISIC 2019 dataset; fine-tuned on SGH dataset.

learning_rate_schedule.initial_training: Custom three-phase schedule: linear ramp-up from 10^-4 to 10^-3 over 3 epochs, sustain phase at 10^-3 for 2 epochs, exponential decay toward 10^-6
learning_rate_schedule.fine_tuning: ReduceLROnPlateau with initial learning rate of 10^-5, reduced by a factor of 0.1 if validation loss did not improve for 10 epochs

regularization_callbacks: Keras EarlyStopping callback (stopped training if validation AUC did not improve for 30 consecutive epochs)

classification_or_output_head.thresholding: Empirically determined based on clinical requirements (sensitivity vs specificity)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing input images to 260 × 260 pixels to meet EfficientNetB2 requirements
Normalization of pixel values by dividing by 255 to reduce inter-image variations caused by brightness and contrast changes
Cropping black frames from the original images in the Asian skin (SGH) dataset
conclusions: AI models trained on predominantly Caucasian populations do not perform equally well on Asian skin of Fitzpatrick types II-III.
The EfficientNetB2 model achieved high performance on Caucasian datasets (AUC of 0.90 on ISIC 2019 and 0.91 on PH2) but performance dropped to an AUC of 0.81 when tested on the Asian SGH dataset.
Fine-tuning the model on the small Asian dataset (SGH) did not improve performance (mean AUC reduced to 0.78), likely due to the small sample size (153 images, only 6 positive samples).
Existing AI-based methods for skin cancer detection may not be optimized for Asian skin, highlighting the need for specific research and data representation for skin of color.
The study is limited by the lack of publicly available large datasets of dermoscopic images containing skin cancer in Asian populations.
The ethnicity of the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets was not explicitly reported but inferred from geographical regions, underscoring the importance of recording ethnicity in such studies.
The results may be data-specific and require rigorous testing with different models and multiple datasets before being firmly established.
important_remarks: The study demonstrates a significant performance drop (AUC 0.90/0.91 to 0.81) when an EfficientNetB2 model trained on predominantly Caucasian datasets (ISIC 2019, PH2) is applied to an Asian dataset (SGH), highlighting racial bias in current AI dermatology models.

The SGH dataset used for Asian skin evaluation is extremely small (153 images) and severely imbalanced (only 6 positive skin cancer cases), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results for Asian populations.
Fine-tuning the model on the small SGH dataset failed to improve performance (AUC dropped to 0.78), likely due to insufficient sample size for effective transfer learning.
The ethnicity of the ISIC 2019 and PH2 datasets was not explicitly reported but inferred from geographical regions, representing a limitation in verifying the racial composition of the training data.
The results may be data-specific; the authors note that rigorous testing with different models and multiple datasets is required before firmly establishing these findings.
There is a critical lack of publicly available large-scale dermoscopic datasets containing skin cancer in Asian populations, which hinders the development and validation of equitable AI models.
